1:
"Now Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children: and she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar." The witnesses note Sarai's barrenness and her Egyptian handmaid Hagar. On "Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children," the grammatical voices note the barrenness. Ellicott and Gill note that "Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children" — Sarai remained barren (11:30), childless after ten years in Canaan (v.3); the long delay of the promised seed; the trial of faith that pressed on Abram and Sarai; the human impossibility against which God's promise stood.

On "she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the handmaid. Calvin and Gill note that Sarai had "an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar" — Hagar, an Egyptian servant (perhaps acquired during the sojourn in Egypt, 12:16); the maid whom Sarai would give to Abram (v.2); the occasion of the trouble that follows.

On the barrenness and the handmaid, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the setting: Sarai's barrenness and the presence of an Egyptian maid set the stage for the expedient (v.2) — Sarai's plan to obtain children by Hagar; the impatience of waiting for the promise; the setting for the birth of Ishmael and the long trial before Isaac. The verse displays Sarai's barrenness and her handmaid Hagar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the barrenness — "Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children," Sarai childless, the delay of the promise (11:30, Ellicott, Gill); "she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar," Hagar the Egyptian maid (12:16, Calvin, Gill); the setting for the expedient (v.2, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the barren Sarai. The verse marks the Sarai: barren. The verse displays the handmaid: Hagar. The verse displays the Egypt: her origin. The verse displays the setting: for the expedient. The barrenness displays the trial of the delayed promise. The verse displays the Sarai: childless. The verse displays the Hagar: her maid. The verse displays the trial: pressing. Sarai's barrenness and her handmaid Hagar display the setting of the trial: Sarai bare no children, and she had an Egyptian handmaid, Hagar — the barren wife, in the long delay of the promise, and the maid who would be the occasion of the expedient and the trouble. The verse displays the barren Sarai, who gives Hagar to Abram (v.2).
2:
"And Sarai said unto Abram, Behold now, the LORD hath restrained me from bearing: I pray thee, go in unto my maid; it may be that I may obtain children by her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai." The witnesses note Sarai's expedient of giving Hagar to Abram — the crux of the act. On "the LORD hath restrained me from bearing," the grammatical voices note Sarai's reasoning. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD hath restrained me from bearing" — Sarai acknowledged that the LORD had withheld children from her; yet, instead of waiting in faith for God to give the seed, she devised a plan to obtain children through her maid; her reasoning mixed a recognition of God's hand with an impatient expedient.

On "go in unto my maid; it may be that I may obtain children by her," interpreters divide over the act. The reformed Calvin and Gill, with many, hold it a fault — a failure of faith and patience: Sarai, weary of waiting for the promise (12:2; 15:4), took matters into her own hands, giving Hagar to Abram to obtain a child by her (the child to be reckoned as Sarai's, by the custom of the time); the expedient sprang from unbelief and impatience, not waiting on God's way and time; it led to strife and trouble (vv.4-6). A second reading (noting the custom) excuses or mitigates the act: the giving of a handmaid to bear children for a barren wife was a recognized custom of the age (later seen in Rachel and Leah, ch. 30); Sarai acted by the custom of her time, with good intent (to obtain the promised seed); the polygamy, though against the original institution (2:24), was countenanced by the custom and not yet plainly forbidden.

On "Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note Abram's compliance: "Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai" — Abram yielded to Sarai's proposal (as Adam to Eve, 3:17); his compliance, too, was a failure of faith — taking the seed by human contrivance rather than waiting for God; the patriarch, with Sarai, leaned on his own way; the consequences (Ishmael, and the strife) followed. The verse displays Sarai's expedient of giving Hagar to Abram.

The witnesses agree the substance: Sarai, barren, gave Hagar to Abram to obtain children, and Abram complied (Keil, Gill, Calvin, Henry). The act bears a crux: a fault of unbelief and impatience — not waiting on God's promise, taking the seed by human contrivance (Calvin, Gill) vs an excusable expedient by the custom of the age — the giving of a handmaid for a barren wife, with good intent (the custom-reading). The verse displays the Hagar-expedient crux. The verse marks the restraint: the LORD's. The verse displays the debate: fault of unbelief, or custom of the age. The verse displays the maid: given. The verse displays the compliance: Abram's. The expedient displays Abram and Sarai taking the seed by contrivance. The verse displays the Sarai: contriving. The verse displays the Abram: hearkening. The verse displays the faith: failing. Sarai's expedient of giving Hagar to Abram displays the trial and a crux: the LORD hath restrained me; go in unto my maid — read as a fault of unbelief and impatience, not waiting on God's promise (Calvin, Gill), or as an excusable custom of the age; Abram hearkening to the voice of Sarai, the seed sought by human contrivance. The verse displays the expedient, with Hagar given as wife (v.3).
3:
"And Sarai Abram's wife took Hagar her maid the Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife." The brief verse notes Sarai giving Hagar to Abram as wife after ten years in Canaan. The grammatical voices note the act. Gill and Ellicott note that "Sarai Abram's wife took Hagar her maid the Egyptian... and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife" — Sarai formally gave Hagar to Abram as a (secondary) wife; the maid raised to the place of a wife, to bear the desired seed; the expedient (v.2) carried out.

On "after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the delay. Calvin and Gill note that this was "after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan" — ten years had passed since the promise (12:2) and the entry into Canaan, yet no child; the long delay that wearied Sarai's patience and prompted the expedient; the trial of faith through the years of waiting.

On "gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the secondary marriage: "to be his wife" — Hagar became Abram's wife (a concubine, a secondary wife); the polygamy that, though by the custom of the age, was against the original institution (2:24); the union that would bring strife (vv.4-6) and the birth of Ishmael; the human contrivance for the seed. The verse displays Sarai giving Hagar to Abram as wife.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "Sarai... took Hagar her maid... and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife," Sarai giving Hagar as a wife (Gill, Ellicott); "after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan," the ten years' delay (Calvin, Gill); "to be his wife," the secondary marriage, against the original institution (2:24, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the given Hagar. The verse marks the Sarai: giving. The verse displays the Hagar: as wife. The verse displays the years: ten. The verse displays the polygamy: introduced. The act displays Sarai raising Hagar to a wife. The verse displays the Hagar: given. The verse displays the delay: ten years. The verse displays the marriage: secondary. Sarai giving Hagar to Abram as wife displays the expedient carried out: after ten years in Canaan, Sarai gave Hagar to Abram to be his wife — the maid raised to a wife to bear the seed, the polygamy of the expedient, against the original institution. The verse displays the given Hagar, who conceives and despises Sarai (v.4).
4:
"And he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived: and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes." The witnesses note Hagar's conceiving and her despising of Sarai. On "he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived," the grammatical voices note the conception. Ellicott and Gill note that "he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived" — Abram took Hagar, and she conceived; the maid, who had been barren-mistress's hope, was now with child; the conception that would breed pride in Hagar and strife in the household.

On "when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the pride. Henry and Gill note that "when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes" — Hagar, finding herself with child (which Sarai was not), grew proud and despised her barren mistress; the maid lifted up against the wife; the pride and insolence of Hagar; the bitter fruit of the expedient — the very plan to help brought contempt and strife.

On the despising, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the trouble: Hagar's contempt of Sarai was the first bitter fruit of the polygamous expedient (v.2); the strife and jealousy that polygamy breeds; Sarai, who had given Hagar, now suffered her scorn; the household disturbed; the consequence of leaning on human contrivance rather than God's promise. The verse displays Hagar's conceiving and her despising of Sarai.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived," Hagar with child (Ellicott, Gill); "her mistress was despised in her eyes," Hagar's pride and contempt of barren Sarai (Henry, Gill); the despising the bitter fruit of the expedient (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the proud Hagar. The verse marks the conception: Hagar's. The verse displays the despising: of Sarai. The verse displays the pride: of the maid. The verse displays the strife: begun. The conception displays Hagar lifted up against her mistress. The verse displays the Hagar: with child. The verse displays the Sarai: despised. The verse displays the trouble: begun. Hagar's conceiving and her despising of Sarai display the bitter fruit of the expedient: Hagar conceived, and despised her barren mistress — the maid grown proud against the wife, the strife and contempt that the polygamous contrivance bred in the household. The verse displays the despised Sarai, who complains to Abram (v.5).
5:
"And Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee: I have given my maid into thy bosom; and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes: the LORD judge between me and thee." The witnesses note Sarai's complaint to Abram over Hagar's contempt. On "My wrong be upon thee," the grammatical voices note the complaint. Gill and Ellicott note that "Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee" — Sarai complained bitterly to Abram, laying the blame (the wrong, the injury she suffered from Hagar's contempt) upon him; the jealousy and grief of the despised wife; Sarai, who had devised the expedient (v.2), now turned the blame on Abram.

On "I have given my maid into thy bosom... I was despised in her eyes," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the grievance. Calvin and Gill note that Sarai recounted her grievance — "I have given my maid into thy bosom; and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes"; she had given Hagar, and now suffered the maid's scorn; the bitter fruit of her own plan turned against her; the passion and inconsistency of Sarai (blaming Abram for what she herself had contrived).

On "the LORD judge between me and thee," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the appeal: "the LORD judge between me and thee" — Sarai appealed to God to judge between her and Abram; the heat of her complaint; the strife within the household, brought before God; the trouble that the expedient had bred, now disturbing the peace of Abram and Sarai; the appeal of the aggrieved wife. The verse displays Sarai's complaint to Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the complaint — "My wrong be upon thee," Sarai blaming Abram for Hagar's contempt (Gill, Ellicott); "I have given my maid into thy bosom... I was despised in her eyes," the grievance of the despised wife (Calvin, Gill); "the LORD judge between me and thee," Sarai's appeal to God (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the complaining Sarai. The verse marks the wrong: laid on Abram. The verse displays the maid: given. The verse displays the despising: of Sarai. The verse displays the appeal: to the LORD. The complaint displays Sarai's grief turned on Abram. The verse displays the Sarai: complaining. The verse displays the blame: on Abram. The verse displays the strife: in the house. Sarai's complaint to Abram over Hagar's contempt displays the strife of the expedient: my wrong be upon thee; I gave my maid, and am despised; the LORD judge — the despised wife, in passion, blaming Abram for the fruit of her own plan, the household troubled. The verse displays the complaining Sarai, answered by Abram (v.6).
6:
"But Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her as it pleaseth thee. And when Sarai dealt hardly with her, she fled from her face." The witnesses note Abram yielding Hagar to Sarai and Hagar's flight. On "Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her as it pleaseth thee," the grammatical voices note Abram's deferral. Gill and Clarke note that "Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her as it pleaseth thee" — Abram, to keep peace, gave Sarai authority over Hagar (the maid still being Sarai's servant); he left Hagar to Sarai's disposal; the patriarch's wish to avoid strife, yielding the matter to his wife.

On "when Sarai dealt hardly with her," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the harshness. Calvin and Gill note that "Sarai dealt hardly with her (afflicted, humbled her)" — Sarai, given the power, treated Hagar harshly (perhaps with excessive severity, in the heat of her jealousy); the rough usage of the proud maid; Sarai's hard dealing, though provoked, not without fault; the affliction that drove Hagar to flee.

On "she fled from her face," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the flight: "she fled from her face" — Hagar, unable to bear Sarai's harsh treatment, fled from her mistress; the proud maid now a fugitive; the flight into the wilderness (v.7), where the angel of the LORD would find her; the trouble of the household driving Hagar away. The verse displays Abram yielding Hagar to Sarai and Hagar's flight.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deferral — "Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her as it pleaseth thee," Abram leaving Hagar to Sarai (Gill, Clarke); "Sarai dealt hardly with her," Sarai's harsh treatment of Hagar (Calvin, Gill); "she fled from her face," Hagar's flight from the harshness (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fleeing Hagar. The verse marks the maid: in Sarai's hand. The verse displays the dealing: hard. The verse displays the Hagar: fled. The verse displays the strife: its fruit. The deferral displays Abram yielding the matter and Hagar fleeing. The verse displays the Abram: deferring. The verse displays the Sarai: harsh. The verse displays the Hagar: fleeing. Abram yielding Hagar to Sarai and Hagar's flight display the trouble of the expedient: Abram left Hagar in Sarai's hand, Sarai dealt hardly with her, and she fled — the patriarch yielding to keep peace, the harsh treatment of the proud maid, and her flight into the wilderness. The verse displays the fleeing Hagar, found by the angel of the LORD (v.7).
7:
"And the angel of the LORD found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur." The witnesses note the angel of the LORD finding Hagar — the crux of the angel's identity. On "the angel of the LORD found her," interpreters expound the identity. The reformed Gill and the dominant Christian reading hold that "the angel of the LORD" here is not a created angel but a divine Person — the uncreated Angel, the Logos or Son of God (a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ): the Angel speaks as God ("I will multiply thy seed," v.10, which only God can do), is called and worshiped as Jehovah by Hagar ("Thou God seest me," v.13), and accepts the name; had he been a created angel, he would have refused the divine honor (as Revelation 19:10; 22:9); the dominant view sees here a theophany, the Angel of the LORD who is identified with the LORD himself. A second reading (the modern/critical, and some) takes the angel of the LORD as a created angel who speaks and acts as God's representative, in God's name (the messenger bearing the divine word and authority).

On "found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the grace. Henry and Gill note that the Angel "found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur" — the fugitive Hagar, fleeing toward Egypt (Shur, on the way to Egypt), was found by the Angel at a fountain in the desert; the grace of God seeking out the afflicted, wandering maid; the LORD's care for Hagar in her distress.

On the finding, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the providence: the Angel of the LORD found Hagar — God's gracious seeking of the lost and afflicted; the providence that met the fleeing maid in the wilderness; the divine condescension to comfort and direct her (vv.8-12); the LORD who sees and seeks the wandering. The verse displays the angel of the LORD finding Hagar.

The witnesses agree the substance: the angel of the LORD found Hagar in the wilderness and spoke to her as God (Gill, Henry, Clarke). The angel's identity bears a crux: a divine Person, the uncreated Angel, the Logos/Son of God (a pre-incarnate Christ/theophany), since he speaks as God and is called Jehovah (the dominant Christian reading — Gill, Clarke) vs a created angel speaking in God's name as his representative (the alternative reading). The verse displays the angel-of-the-LORD crux. The verse marks the Angel: found her. The verse displays the debate: divine Person or created angel. The verse displays the fountain: in the wilderness. The verse displays the grace: seeking. The finding displays the Angel of the LORD meeting the fugitive Hagar. The verse displays the Angel: of the LORD. The verse displays the Hagar: found. The verse displays the grace: of God. The angel of the LORD finding Hagar displays the gracious seeking and a crux: the angel of the LORD found her by a fountain in the wilderness — the Angel read as a divine Person, the uncreated Angel, the Son of God (since he speaks as God and is called Jehovah, the dominant view) or as a created angel in God's name; God's grace seeking the afflicted, wandering maid. The verse displays the found Hagar, questioned by the Angel (v.8).
8:
"And he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go? And she said, I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai." The witnesses note the Angel's questioning of Hagar. On "Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go?" the grammatical voices note the question. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go?" — the Angel addressed Hagar by name and by her station ("Sarai's maid," recalling her duty), and asked where she came from and where she was going; the question not for information (the Angel knew) but to bring Hagar to reflection — to own her situation and consider her course; the gracious dealing that leads the wanderer to self-examination.

On "Sarai's maid," the puritan Henry notes the reminder. Henry and Gill note that the Angel called her "Sarai's maid" — reminding Hagar of her place and relation (still Sarai's servant); the title that recalled her duty and her flight from it; the gentle reproof in the address.

On "I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the confession: "I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai" — Hagar confessed she was fleeing from Sarai; she owned her flight (though she could not say whither she was going, having no sure refuge); the honest acknowledgment drawn out by the Angel's question; the fugitive brought to confess her case, preparing for the Angel's counsel (v.9). The verse displays the Angel's questioning of Hagar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the question — "Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go?" the Angel bringing Hagar to reflection (Keil, Gill); "Sarai's maid," the reminder of her place (Henry, Gill); "I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai," Hagar's confession of her flight (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the questioned Hagar. The verse marks the question: whence and whither. The verse displays the maid: Sarai's. The verse displays the flight: confessed. The verse displays the reflection: drawn out. The questioning displays the Angel leading Hagar to own her case. The verse displays the Angel: asking. The verse displays the Hagar: confessing. The verse displays the flight: owned. The Angel's questioning of Hagar displays the gracious dealing: Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence and whither? and she confessed, I flee from my mistress — the Angel bringing the fugitive to reflection and confession, the reminder of her place preparing for his counsel. The verse displays the questioned Hagar, bidden to return (v.9).
9:
"And the angel of the LORD said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands." The brief verse notes the Angel's command that Hagar return and submit. The grammatical voices note the counsel. Gill and Ellicott note that "the angel of the LORD said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands" — the Angel commanded Hagar to return to Sarai and submit (humble herself) under her authority; not to flee her duty, but to go back and bear her lot patiently; the counsel of God to the fugitive — return and submit.

On "Return to thy mistress," the witnesses note the duty. Gill notes that Hagar was to return to her mistress; the Angel sent her back to her place and duty; the flight from affliction was not the way; God would have her return and endure.

On "submit thyself under her hands," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the submission: "submit thyself under her hands" — Hagar was to humble herself and submit to Sarai's authority (her pride, v.4, having helped breed the strife); the counsel of humility and patience; the servant to bear her lot under her mistress; the duty of submission, with the promise (v.10) to encourage her; God's way for Hagar — not flight, but humble return and submission. The verse displays the Angel's command that Hagar return and submit.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the counsel — "Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands," the Angel commanding Hagar to return and submit (Gill, Ellicott); "Return to thy mistress," the duty to go back (Gill); "submit thyself under her hands," the counsel of humility and patience (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the counseled Hagar. The verse marks the command: return. The verse displays the submission: under Sarai. The verse displays the duty: to bear her lot. The verse displays the humility: counseled. The command displays the Angel sending Hagar back to submit. The verse displays the Hagar: to return. The verse displays the pride: humbled. The verse displays the duty: pressed. The Angel's command that Hagar return and submit displays God's counsel to the fugitive: return to thy mistress and submit under her hands — not flight from affliction, but humble return and submission, the way of patience, with the promise to encourage her. The verse displays the counseled Hagar, given the promise (v.10).
10:
"And the angel of the LORD said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, that it shall not be numbered for multitude." The witnesses note the Angel's promise to multiply Hagar's seed. On "I will multiply thy seed exceedingly," the grammatical voices note the divine promise. Ellicott and Gill note that "the angel of the LORD said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly" — the Angel promised to multiply Hagar's seed (through Ishmael); notably, the Angel speaks as God ("I will multiply"), a promise only God can make and fulfill — evidence (with v.13) that the Angel is a divine Person; the promise of a numerous posterity to the afflicted maid.

On "I will multiply," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the divine speech. Calvin and Gill note that the Angel's saying "I will multiply thy seed" — taking to himself the divine work — confirms that he is the LORD himself (the uncreated Angel, v.7); the promise spoken in God's own person; the divine assurance to Hagar.

On "that it shall not be numbered for multitude," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the multitude: "that it shall not be numbered for multitude" — Hagar's seed (the Ishmaelites, the Arab peoples) would be innumerable; the promise of a great multitude of descendants (fulfilled in the many Arab nations, 25:12-18; 17:20, twelve princes); the comfort and encouragement given to Hagar to return (v.9); the blessing on the afflicted maid and her son. The verse displays the Angel's promise to multiply Hagar's seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "I will multiply thy seed exceedingly," the Angel promising a numerous seed, speaking as God (Ellicott, Gill); "I will multiply," the divine speech confirming the Angel is the LORD (v.7, Calvin, Gill); "that it shall not be numbered for multitude," the innumerable Ishmaelite/Arab peoples (17:20; 25:12-18, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised seed. The verse marks the seed: multiplied. The verse displays the Angel: speaks as God. The verse displays the multitude: innumerable. The verse displays the comfort: to Hagar. The promise displays the Angel assuring Hagar of a great posterity. The verse displays the seed: countless. The verse displays the Angel: divine. The verse displays the comfort: given. The Angel's promise to multiply Hagar's seed displays the divine assurance: I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, beyond numbering — the Angel, speaking as God, promising the afflicted maid an innumerable posterity (the Arab peoples, 17:20; 25:12-18), the comfort to encourage her return. The verse displays the promised seed, named Ishmael (v.11).
11:
"And the angel of the LORD said unto her, Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael; because the LORD hath heard thy affliction." The witnesses note the naming of Ishmael, "God hears." On "Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son," the grammatical voices note the announcement. Gill and Ellicott note that "Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael" — the Angel announced that Hagar would bear a son and should name him Ishmael; the foretelling of the birth and the giving of the name; the comfort to the afflicted maid.

On "shalt call his name Ishmael," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the name. Clarke and Gill note that "Ishmael" means "God shall hear" (or "God hears"); the name commemorating God's hearing of Hagar's affliction; the son named for the mercy of God to his mother; the meaning of the name a witness to God's regard.

On "because the LORD hath heard thy affliction," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the mercy: "because the LORD hath heard thy affliction" — the name Ishmael ("God hears") was given because the LORD had heard (regarded) Hagar's affliction; God's gracious notice of the suffering maid; the comfort that God hears the afflicted and the cry of the distressed; the LORD's mercy to Hagar in her trouble. The verse displays the naming of Ishmael.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the announcement — "Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael," the foretelling and naming (Gill, Ellicott); "Ishmael," "God hears" (Clarke, Gill); "because the LORD hath heard thy affliction," God's notice of Hagar's suffering (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named Ishmael. The verse marks the son: to be born. The verse displays the name: Ishmael. The verse displays the meaning: God hears. The verse displays the affliction: heard. The naming displays God's mercy to the afflicted Hagar. The verse displays the Ishmael: named. The verse displays the LORD: heard. The verse displays the affliction: regarded. The naming of Ishmael displays God's gracious hearing: thou shalt bear a son and call him Ishmael, for the LORD hath heard thy affliction — the son named "God hears," a witness that God regarded the suffering of the afflicted maid. The verse displays the named Ishmael, whose character is foretold (v.12).
12:
"And he will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren." The witnesses note the prophecy of Ishmael's wild and independent character. On "he will be a wild man," the grammatical voices note the description. Gill and Clarke note that "he will be a wild man (a wild-ass man, pere adam)" — Ishmael would be like the wild ass (Job 39:5-8), free, untamed, roving the desert; the description of a wild, free, nomadic people; the character of Ishmael and his descendants (the Bedouin Arabs), wild and independent.

On "his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the conflict. Clarke and Ellicott note that "his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him" — Ishmael's posterity would be a warlike, contentious people, at strife with their neighbors and resisted by them; the marauding, plundering life of the desert peoples; the perpetual conflict of the Ishmaelite/Arab tribes; the prophecy fulfilled in the history of the Arabs.

On "he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the independence: "he shall dwell in the presence (in the face, or to the east) of all his brethren" — Ishmael would dwell near, yet independent of, his brethren (the other descendants of Abraham, Isaac's line and Keturah's); the Ishmaelites maintaining their place and independence among the nations; the wild, free people dwelling in the sight of, but distinct from, their kindred; the prophecy of the enduring, independent Arab peoples. The verse displays the prophecy of Ishmael's character.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the description — "he will be a wild man," Ishmael like the wild ass, free and untamed (Job 39:5-8, Gill, Clarke); "his hand against every man," the warlike, contentious Ishmaelites (Clarke, Ellicott); "he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren," the independent Arab peoples dwelling near yet distinct (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wild Ishmael. The verse marks the man: wild. The verse displays the hand: against all. The verse displays the dwelling: among his brethren. The verse displays the prophecy: of the Arabs. The description displays Ishmael's wild, independent posterity. The verse displays the Ishmael: wild. The verse displays the conflict: perpetual. The verse displays the independence: enduring. The prophecy of Ishmael's wild and independent character displays the Arab peoples: he will be a wild man, his hand against every man, dwelling in the presence of all his brethren — the free, untamed, warlike, and enduring posterity of Ishmael (the Arabs), fulfilled in their history. The verse displays the foretold Ishmael, and Hagar's naming of God (v.13).
13:
"And she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me: for she said, Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?" The witnesses note Hagar's naming of God, "Thou God seest me" — the El-roi crux. On "she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me," the grammatical voices note the naming. Ellicott and Gill note that "she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me (El-roi, the God who sees me)" — Hagar named the LORD (whom she recognized in the Angel, v.7) "El-roi," the God of seeing/the God who sees; she confessed that God had seen and regarded her in her affliction; the afflicted maid's confession of the all-seeing God who beheld her in the wilderness; notably, Hagar calls the Angel "the LORD" and "God" — confirming the Angel's divine identity (v.7).

On "Thou God seest me," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the seeing God. Calvin and Gill note that "Thou God seest me" — Hagar found that the eye of God was upon her, seeing her affliction and her flight; the comfort that God sees the afflicted; the name confessing God's gracious, watchful regard; the wonder that God had seen and sought her.

On "Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?" interpreters note the difficult phrase. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the difficulty: "Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?" — the Hebrew is difficult and variously rendered; the sense is taken either as Hagar's wonder that she had here seen (the back of) God (the Angel) and yet lived (as men feared to see God and die, 32:30; Exodus 33:20); or as her marveling that she had looked after (sought, regarded) the God who sees her; or that even here, in the wilderness, she had seen the God who saw her; the difficult clause expressing Hagar's awe at the divine appearance and the seeing God. The verse displays Hagar's naming of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Hagar named the LORD (the Angel) "El-roi," the God who sees, confessing his gracious regard of her affliction (Ellicott, Gill, Calvin). The clause "Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?" bears a crux of the difficult Hebrew: read as Hagar's wonder that she had seen God (the Angel) and lived (cf. 32:30; Exodus 33:20) vs her marveling at the God who sees her, whom she had here beheld; and the naming confirms the Angel's divine identity (v.7). The verse displays the El-roi crux. The verse marks the name: El-roi, God sees. The verse displays the debate: the difficult clause. The verse displays the seeing: God's. The verse displays the Angel: confessed as God. The naming displays Hagar's confession of the all-seeing God. The verse displays the Hagar: naming. The verse displays the God: who sees. The verse displays the awe: at his appearing. Hagar's naming of God displays the seeing God and a crux: she called the LORD El-roi, Thou God seest me, saying, Have I here looked after him that seeth me? — the afflicted maid confessing the all-seeing God who beheld her, the difficult clause read as wonder that she had seen God and lived or marveling at the God who sees her, the Angel confessed as the LORD (v.7). The verse displays the named well, Beer-lahai-roi (v.14).
14:
"Wherefore the well was called Beerlahairoi; behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered." The brief verse notes the naming of the well Beer-lahai-roi. The grammatical voices note the name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the well was called Beerlahairoi (the well of the living one who sees me, or the well of him that liveth and seeth me); behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered" — the well by which the Angel found Hagar (v.7) was named Beer-lahai-roi, commemorating the appearance of the living God who saw and spoke to her; the name a memorial of God's gracious meeting with Hagar; the well located between Kadesh and Bered (in the wilderness toward Shur).

On "Beerlahairoi," the witnesses note the memorial. Gill notes that the name "Beer-lahai-roi" (variously rendered, "the well of the living one that seeth me," or "the well of him that liveth and seeth me") commemorated Hagar's confession (v.13) that the living God had seen her; the well a standing memorial of God's mercy to the afflicted maid; the place named for the divine encounter.

On "between Kadesh and Bered," the grammatical-historical note the location: "between Kadesh and Bered" — the well lay in the wilderness, between Kadesh and Bered (places in the south, toward Shur and Egypt); the geographical note locating the place; the well that Isaac would later dwell by (24:62; 25:11); the memorial of God's grace, a known place in the land. The verse displays the naming of the well Beer-lahai-roi.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "the well was called Beerlahairoi," the well named for the living God who sees (Keil, Gill); "Beer-lahai-roi," the memorial of God's seeing Hagar (v.13, Gill); "between Kadesh and Bered," the location in the wilderness (24:62, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named well. The verse marks the well: Beer-lahai-roi. The verse displays the name: the living God who sees. The verse displays the place: between Kadesh and Bered. The verse displays the memorial: of grace. The naming displays the well commemorating God's meeting with Hagar. The verse displays the well: named. The verse displays the God: who sees. The verse displays the place: located. The naming of the well Beer-lahai-roi displays the memorial of God's grace: the well was called Beer-lahai-roi, the well of the living one who sees me — commemorating Hagar's meeting with the living God who saw her, a standing memorial in the wilderness. The verse displays the named well, by which Hagar bears Ishmael (v.15).
15:
"And Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare, Ishmael." The brief verse notes the birth and naming of Ishmael. The grammatical voices note the birth. Gill and Henry note that "Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare, Ishmael" — Hagar (having returned and submitted, v.9) bore Abram a son, and Abram named him Ishmael (as the Angel had directed, v.11); the birth of Ishmael, Abram's first son; the naming according to God's word.

On "Hagar bare Abram a son," the witnesses note the son. Gill notes that Hagar bore the son foretold (v.11); Abram, at last, had a son (though not the son of promise); the birth of Ishmael, the fruit of the expedient (v.2).

On "Abram called his son's name... Ishmael," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the naming: "Abram called his son's name... Ishmael" — Abram named the child Ishmael ("God hears," v.11), in obedience to the Angel's word; (Hagar must have told Abram of the Angel's appearance and the name); the naming according to the divine direction; the son named for God's hearing of Hagar's affliction. The verse displays the birth and naming of Ishmael.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "Hagar bare Abram a son," Hagar bearing the foretold son (v.11, Gill, Henry); Abram's first son, the fruit of the expedient (Gill); "Abram called his son's name... Ishmael," the naming per the Angel's word (v.11, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Ishmael. The verse marks the Hagar: bore. The verse displays the son: Ishmael. The verse displays the naming: per God's word. The verse displays the Abram: first son. The birth displays Ishmael born and named as foretold. The verse displays the Hagar: a son. The verse displays the Ishmael: named. The verse displays the obedience: in naming. The birth and naming of Ishmael display the fulfillment of the Angel's word: Hagar bore a son, and Abram named him Ishmael — the son foretold (v.11) born and named according to the divine direction, Abram's first son. The verse displays the born Ishmael, with Abram's age (v.16).
16:
"And Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram." The very brief verse notes Abram's age at Ishmael's birth. The grammatical voices note the date. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abram was fourscore and six years old (eighty-six), when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram" — Abram was eighty-six at Ishmael's birth; the date noted, marking the time of the birth; Abram had been eleven years in Canaan (entering at 75, 12:4); the dating of Ishmael's birth, thirteen years before the covenant of circumcision (17:1, 25, when Abram was 99 and Ishmael 13).

On "fourscore and six years old," the witnesses note the chronology. Gill notes that Abram's age (86) at Ishmael's birth fixes the chronology; the long wait (eleven years in Canaan) before even this son; and the further wait (to Abram's 100th year, 21:5) before Isaac, the son of promise.

On the dating, the grammatical-historical note the interval: the noting of Abram's age (86) marks the interval before the next great event — the covenant of circumcision and the promise of Isaac (ch. 17), thirteen years later (Abram 99); the chronology of the patriarch's life; the long trial of faith between the promise and its fulfillment in Isaac. The verse displays Abram's age at Ishmael's birth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the date — "Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram," Abram eighty-six at Ishmael's birth (Keil, Gill); the chronology, eleven years in Canaan (12:4, Gill); the interval to the covenant (17:1, 25, thirteen years later, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dated birth. The verse marks the age: 86. The verse displays the Ishmael: born. The verse displays the chronology: fixed. The verse displays the interval: to ch. 17. The dating displays Abram's age at Ishmael's birth. The verse displays the Abram: 86. The verse displays the Ishmael: born. The verse displays the wait: continuing. Abram's age at Ishmael's birth displays the chronology of the patriarch: Abram was eighty-six when Hagar bore Ishmael — the dated birth, eleven years into the sojourn in Canaan, thirteen years before the covenant of circumcision and the promise of Isaac (ch. 17). The verse closes the chapter, before the covenant (ch.17).
